relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus continues through parable.
Heaven’s reign is pictured as a wedding feast for the son.
The first invited refuse the feast.
Everything is ready, and the invitation is repeated.
Indifference turns people back to field and trade.
Some answer invitation with violence.
Rejected grace becomes judgment on the violent city.
The feast remains ready though the first invited are unworthy.
The invitation moves outward to the crossroads.
The hall fills with all kinds of guests.
The king discerns whether the guest is clothed for the feast.
Friend is speechless before the missing garment.
The warning is serious: invitation still calls for transformation.
Many are called, few are chosen.
The Pharisees plot to trap him in words.
Flattery becomes the setup for testing.
They ask about Caesar’s tribute.
Jesus names the test beneath the question.
He asks for the coin.
Image and inscription reveal the claim.
Caesar receives what bears Caesar’s image; the Divine receives what bears divine image.
They marvel and leave.
Sadducees question him from denial of resurrection.
They cite Moses and levirate duty.
They build a case around seven brothers.
The chain continues through the seventh.
The woman also dies.
They ask whose wife she will be in resurrection.
Jesus says they miss Scripture and divine power.
Resurrection life is not merely present arrangements extended.
He turns them back to the Divine speaking in Scripture.
The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is God of the living.
The crowds are astonished.
The Pharisees regroup after the Sadducees are silenced.
A legal expert tests him.
He asks for the great command.
Love of the Divine is whole-heart, whole-life, whole-mind.
This is the first great command.
Neighbor-love is its companion.
All Law and Prophets hang from these two loves.
Jesus now questions them.
He asks whose son Messiah is.
David speaking in Spirit complicates a flat answer.
The Lord speaks to David’s Lord at the right hand.
The son of David is also David’s Lord.
The questioning falls silent.
